entries.title: On Luke 1:28–29
entries.excerpt: Mark the virgin by her manner of life. Alone in an inner chamber, unseen by the eyes of men, discovered only by an angel; as it is said, And the angel came in unto her. That she might not be dishonoured by any ignoble address, she is saluted by an angel.

entries.title: On Luke 1:28–29
entries.excerpt: Far different then to the news formerly addressed to the woman, is the announcement now made to the Virgin. In the former, the cause of sin was punished by the pains of childbirth; in the latter, through gladness, sorrow is driven away. Hence the angel not unaptly proclaims joy to the Virgin, saying, Hail.

entries.title: On Luke 1:28–29
entries.excerpt: But mark the Virgin by her bashfulness, for she was afraid, as it follows; And when she heard, she was troubled, It is the habit of virgins to tremble, and to be ever afraid at the presence of man, and to be shy when he addresses her. Learn, O virgin, to avoid light talking. Mary feared even the salutation of an angel.

entries.title: On Luke 1:28–29
entries.excerpt: She wondered also at the new form of blessing, unheard of before, reserved for Mary alone.

entries.title: On Luke 1:28–29
entries.excerpt: For if Mary had known that similar words had been addressed to others, such a salutation would never have appeared to her so strange and alarming.

entries.title: On Luke 1:28–29
entries.excerpt: And it is well said, Full of grace, for to others, grace comes in part; into Mary at once the fulness of grace wholly infused itself. She truly is full of grace through whom has been poured forth upon every creature the abundant rain of the Holy Spirit. But already He was with the Virgin Who sent the angel to the Virgin. The Lord preceded His messenger, for He could not be confined by place Who dwells in all places. Whence it follows, The Lord is with thee.

entries.title: On Luke 1:28–29
entries.excerpt: More than with me, for He Himself is in thy heart, He is (made) in thy womb, He fills thy soul, He fills thy womb.
